dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 7 responding for seizures at --Address Redacted--.
Hi ma'am, I'm Javier, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 tonight?
She's having a seizure again. She's been seizing all day.
Ma'am can you tell me your name and what day it is?
She's looking at us when we touched her. Eye opening is observed.
She said, "I don't know what day it is."
When calm, she will sit up. When agitated, she kicks and elbowed the AIC earlier.
Airway is patent and self-maintained. No signs of obstruction observed.
Resp rate is 17. Breathing non-labored, adequate in depth. Oxygen saturation is 98%.
Pulse is 119, regular and strong. Blood pressure 139 over 81.
Skin is warm and normal in color as per ePCR notes.
Are you experiencing pain or any other symptoms right now?
I have a headache and am very tired. I don't feel good.
What medications do you take regularly, ma'am?
She was on Keppra for her seizures but she stopped taking it because it's expensive.
Are you allergic to any medications or foods?
No, I'm not allergic to anything.
What medical conditions do you have?
She has epilepsy and major depression.

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: Can you tell me about what happened today?

dialogue.micro_intent: S.A.M.P.L.E. events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: She's had seizures all day and drank about six bottles of liquor today. She had tonic-like seizures earlier.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how would you rate the pain from these symptoms today?
No pain. I just feel tired.
Her skin has no visible bruising, bleeding, or rashes.
I need to check your neck and head. Would you be comfortable with that?
No, don't! I feel fine.
We can't perform the full stroke test due to combative behavior.
Radial and pedal pulses are strong and equal. No visible deformity in extremities noted.
Pulse is 119, regular and strong at presentation.
Respiratory rate is 17, non-labored, adequate in depth, and regular depth observed initially.
Blood pressure is 139 over 81, documented at 23:45.
Blood glucose is 91 mg/dL before alcohol effects were evident.
Oxygen saturation is 98% via peripheral pulse oximetry.
For safety, we need to apply temporary restraints. Understandable if you're upset about it, ma'am's reaction is recorded for safety.
Why am I tied down?
For your and our safety. We used cravats. Left arm is above your head, and the right is at your side, as protocol states.
We're now placing you on a cardiac monitor to detect any seizure activity during transport, which is crucial per protocol for safety.
4-lead EKG monitoring is now active and showing documented initial sinus tachycardia at the time recorded.
EKG is showing sinus tachycardia. No life-threatening arrhythmias on initial monitoring.
She is AAOx1/4-alert to person only, disoriented to time and place. She thought she was in Brooklyn and asked, "When did I get home?" upon realizing she was in Richmond.
I'm requesting to place a nasal cannula to monitor breathing. Would you allow us to do that, as per protocol?
Hmm... do what you must. Her initial approval was verbal before removal later.
We're moving you onto the stretcher now. We placed you in a position of comfort and applied five-point restraints, as recorded.
EMS1 and AIC are assisting. We are both here with you with your positioning during this loading phase.
Restraints are secure. You are stable for transport now.
You are now safe and being prepared for the hospital, as told to family members.
What is your preferred hospital destination, as per details provided by your family earlier?
She wants to go to Johnston Willis Hospital. I called them and let them know you're coming.
She removed the nasal cannula, screaming "Let me out!" We are now monitoring breathing via the cardiac monitor, as ePCR notes with resistance against EtCO2 monitoring.
Pulse is now 104, regular and strong. Skin remains warm, no noticeable color changes.
Respiratory rate is 20, non-labored. Breathing seems stable.
Blood pressure has changed to 110/96 now. Documented during subsequent monitoring.
Blood glucose is 0 mg/dL now. This contradicts the first reading but aligns with updated data per ePCR.
Oxygen saturation remains at 98%. No notable deviations during this phase.
EKG is still within sinus tachycardia. No new arrhythmias or artifacts recorded.
We initially measured EtCO2 at 38mmHg, but could not continue due to resistance. We'll monitor for seizures using the cardiac monitor as noted.
Medical care is guided by protocols for seizures and behavioral restraint in this risk context.
She is combative intermittently. No new seizure activity yet. As observed and recorded.
She is resisting but appears less aggressive now since being secured.
The cardiac monitor continues to show stable sinus tachycardia. No abnormalities identified so far.
Hospital staff is aware and preparing for her arrival, as noted in enroute communications sent.
Cindy at JWH, this is Unit 7 with Michael. Seizure case. GCS 14. Alert to person only. She asked "When did I get home?" here in Richmond. Destination is Johnston Willis.
She will be taken to her family's chosen hospital as ePCR indicates.
12-lead EKG was not obtained. She refused it, and it was unsafe to perform during transport due to resistance.
Pain level remains at 0, no pain reported since arrival. As noted per ePCR.
Transport is proceeding as per standard protocol with patient secured.
ED at Johnston Willis is prepared for patient arrival as per hospital contact information.
She will be evaluated carefully upon arrival as intended per care plan.
No additional interventions or statements are needed at this time.
Vitals are consistent and available for review as needed per ePCR data.
All systems are stable. Transport proceeding per protocol guidelines.
Patient is ready for handover upon arrival as noted.
